1: The Decapolis (Ten Cities) was a league of Hellenistic cities east and southeast of the Sea of Galilee — largely Gentile territory. The presence of a large herd of pigs (v.11) confirms the Gentile setting: pig-keeping was forbidden under Levitical law and would not occur in Jewish territory. The encounter with the Gerasene demoniac (the region of the Gerasenes is probably modern Kursi on the eastern shore) is the first extended Gentile healing in Mark, foreshadowing the Gentile mission. The name Legion (v.9) is explicitly Roman: a Roman legion comprised approximately 6,000 soldiers — the demonic power takes the name of the occupying imperial force.
2: The tombs as a dwelling place signal extreme social exclusion: contact with the dead made a person ritually impure for seven days under Levitical law (Num 19:16), and a person who lived among the tombs would be perpetually in a state of uncleanness, unable to enter a village or participate in communal life. The man's habitation in the tombs is not merely an image of isolation but a legal condition — he was excluded from every institution of Jewish society. Approaching Jesus from the tombs underscores what kind of person Jesus was willing to engage.
3: Chains and restraints were the ancient world's primary (and only) response to violent mental disturbance. There was no treatment — only containment. The repeated failures of binding (he tore the chains) would have been read against the background of Second Temple Jewish and Greco-Roman demonology, in which certain possessing spirits were understood to grant superhuman strength. The man's condition was not merely unusual but had exhausted the community's entire capacity to manage him.
4: The detail about broken chains and shattered fetters serves as a narrative proof of the severity of the possession and the inadequacy of human remedies. The community has tried and repeatedly failed. This sets up the contrast: what chains could not accomplish, a word from Jesus will achieve. For Mark's Roman audience, the imagery of inescapable chains suddenly snapped would have registered as a power demonstration of the most concrete kind.
5: Night and day, in the tombs and in the mountains, crying out and cutting himself with stones — the picture is of unrelenting torment with no rest and no audience except the hills. Self-cutting (or self-laceration) was associated in the ancient world with prophetic ecstasy and mourning rituals (cf. 1 Kgs 18:28, where the prophets of Baal cut themselves). Here the act is a symptom of uncontrolled internal violence turned outward. The man had become his own enemy, with no power to stop himself.
6: The demoniac runs and falls in prostration (prosekynēsen) before Jesus — the same gesture of submission that Mark uses for Jairus (5:22), the Syrophoenician woman (7:25), and the disciples at the Transfiguration. The prostration from a distance registers the power differential: the demon-possessed man cannot approach the one before whom the forces controlling him must kneel. This involuntary reverence from the demonic side is a consistent feature of Mark's exorcism accounts (cf. 1:24, 3:11).
7: "Son of the Most High God" (hyie tou theou tou hypsistou) is a Gentile form of divine address. "Most High" (Hypsistos) was a title used in the Hellenistic world for the supreme deity across multiple religious traditions — it appears in Greek inscriptions from Syria and Phoenicia as a generic monotheistic title accessible to both Jews and Gentiles. The adjuration "in God's name don't torture me" follows the pattern of ancient magical adjuration formulas, in which a lesser spiritual power attempts to bind a greater one by invoking a divine name. The demon is trying (and failing) to use Jesus's own God against him.
8: Mark's editorial note — "for Jesus had said to him, 'Come out'" — places the demoniac's prostration and adjuration as a response to an already-issued exorcism command. The demon's elaborate resistance is thus not a negotiation Jesus is open to but a defeated power's final thrashing. This sequence is consistent with Mark's exorcism pattern: the command is given once and is effective; the manifestations of resistance are symptoms of departure, not signs of failure.
9: The name Legion is one of the most politically charged moments in the Gospel. A Roman legion was the primary military unit of the Roman army, comprising approximately 6,000 infantry plus cavalry and support — roughly the garrison that would have been stationed in the region. The demon takes the name of the dominant occupying power. For Mark's Roman audience this naming would have been both bold and recognizable: the forces that tormented this man in the tombs bear the name of the forces that ruled the empire. The irony is structural — Legion, which no one could bind, will be cast into swine.
10: The demons' plea not to be sent "out of the area" (out of the country, chōra) reflects a widespread ancient belief that spiritual powers were territorially bound — each region had its associated spirits, and expulsion from a territory meant displacement into a powerless limbo. This belief appears in various forms in Jewish literature (1 Enoch, Jubilees) and Greco-Roman demonology. Jesus's exorcisms are not merely personal healings but territorial assertions: he has authority over every region he enters, including Gentile Decapolis.
11: The large herd of pigs grazing on the hillside is the clearest possible marker that this is Gentile territory. Leviticus 11:7 prohibited pigs as unclean animals; no observant Jewish community would maintain a pig herd. The Decapolis cities were Hellenistic foundations (many established by Alexander's successors) whose populations were primarily Gentile. The economic scale — two thousand pigs (v.13) — indicates a substantial commercial operation, making the loss not merely dramatic but financially significant for the region.
12: The demons' request to be sent into the pigs rather than expelled entirely reflects the same territorial logic as v.10: they are bargaining for continued presence in the region, even in a degraded host. The pigs are available, numerous, and legally unclean — from the demons' perspective, an acceptable alternative. Jesus grants the request, but the result is not the prolonged presence the demons sought: the pigs rush into the lake and drown. The bargain collapses immediately.
13: The herd of roughly two thousand rushing down the steep bank into the lake echoes the drowning of the Egyptian army in the Sea of Reeds (Exod 14:28) — a connection Mark's audience may or may not have caught, but which belongs to a consistent Exodus-and-new-liberation pattern in the Gospel. The Sea of Galilee (fresh water, not sea) becomes the site of another enemy's destruction. The economic loss for the local pig-herders was considerable — two thousand pigs represents significant capital — which explains the community's response in v.17.
14: The herdsmen flee immediately to report what happened — not just to the town but to the surrounding countryside. The verb "fled" (ephygon) suggests panic, not merely haste. Their report triggers a public response: people come out to see. The scene about to unfold (vv.15-17) is a community confronting the aftermath of an event that exceeds their interpretive categories. They have lost property and gained a formerly dangerous man sitting quietly in his right mind — and they are afraid of the latter more than relieved by it.
15: The transformed man — sitting, clothed, in his right mind — is the visual proof of what happened. "Sitting" (kathēmenon) contrasts with his previous constant restless motion (day and night in the tombs and hills, v.5); "clothed" implies he had been going without clothing, a detail Luke makes explicit (Luke 8:27); "in his right mind" (sōphrona) means rational, self-possessed, capable of conversation. The community's fear at this sight is psychologically precise: what they knew how to manage (the dangerous man) has been replaced by something they do not know how to classify.
16: The eyewitnesses — specifically those who had seen both the exorcism and the destruction of the pig herd — give the full account to the arriving crowd. The double report (what happened to the demoniac and also about the pigs) implies the crowd was already focused on the economic dimension: the pigs mattered to this Gentile community in a way that would not have applied to Jewish territory. The community's subsequent request (v.17) confirms that the economic loss weighs more heavily than the healing in their response to Jesus.
17: The community asks Jesus to leave — not out of hostility to him personally, but out of fear of further disruption. The loss of two thousand pigs was a community-scale economic event. For a Gentile Decapolis village whose livelihood might depend partly on pig production for the urban markets of the Hellenistic cities, this was catastrophic. Their fear is the fear of further unpredictable power, not the reverent fear of the holy. The contrast with the healed man's request (v.18) is deliberate: the community rejects what the individual embraces.
18: The healed man's request to "go with" Jesus (be with him, hina met autou ē) uses the same verb as Mark's description of the Twelve's appointment: Jesus appointed twelve "to be with him" (hina ōsin met autou, 3:14). He is asking for the same relationship the disciples have — not merely gratitude but ongoing proximity to Jesus. Jesus refuses the request, but the refusal is itself a commission: his attachment is redirected, not rejected. The Decapolis becomes his mission field.
19: Jesus's command to "go home to your own people" and report "how much the Lord has done for you" is striking in context. In Jewish territory, Jesus consistently commands silence (the Messianic Secret, 1:44, 5:43, 7:36). Here in Gentile territory he explicitly commissions proclamation — because the Messianic Secret is a specifically Jewish concern (about premature royal-Messianic enthusiasm), not applicable in the Decapolis. The healed man becomes the first missionary figure in Mark, sent to announce what he himself experienced.
20: The Decapolis was a circuit of ten (or more) Hellenistic cities — a confederation including Damascus, Gerasa, Philadelphia, Scythopolis, and others — stretching across modern Jordan and southern Syria. The man proclaims throughout this entire region: a Gentile witness to a Gentile audience, before any of the Twelve have been formally sent on mission. The response ("all were amazed") is the standard Markan reaction to demonstrations of power, but here it is wholly Gentile amazement, outside the synagogue framework entirely.
21: The return crossing to "the other side" brings Jesus back to Jewish territory (the western shore of the Sea of Galilee near Capernaum). The crowd that gathers immediately reflects the pattern of Capernaum and surrounding Galilee: Jesus cannot be in public without drawing a crowd. The double healing narrative that follows (5:21-43) is a classic Markan intercalation: one story is interrupted by another, and the two interpret each other — both involve twelve (twelve years of illness; a twelve-year-old girl), both involve women, both involve the boundary between death and life.
22: Jairus is identified as archisynagōgos — the ruler or president of the synagogue. This was an elected or appointed lay official responsible for managing the synagogue building, maintaining order during worship, and inviting speakers. It was a position of considerable social prestige in a Jewish community. That Jairus comes and falls at Jesus's feet (prosepipton) is a striking social reversal: the synagogue's chief administrative officer prostrates himself before an itinerant Galilean teacher whose authority is not institutionally derived. His daughter's crisis overrides every social calculation.
23: The phrase "little daughter" (to thygatrion mou) uses the diminutive, expressing tender affection. His request is for Jesus to "lay hands on her" (epitithēs tas cheiras) — physical imposition of hands was a recognized healing gesture in Jewish practice, used by rabbis and associated with prophetic blessing. The phrase "so that she will be healed and live" (sōthē kai zēsē) uses vocabulary that in Greek can mean both physical recovery and eschatological life — ambiguity that becomes significant when she dies before Jesus arrives.
24: The crowd pressing around Jesus as he walks — the Greek word sunthlibō (pressed, squeezed) appears only here and in the next verse — sets up the context for the hemorrhagic woman's story. The crowd is physically dense, bodies in contact with Jesus. Yet only one touch, from one specific person with specific intention and need, triggers a transfer of healing power. The crowd as background pressure makes the woman's deliberate touch against the grain of the movement all the more remarkable.
25: A woman with a hemorrhage for twelve years: Leviticus 15:25-27 specified that a woman with an abnormal blood flow was ritually impure for the duration of the flow, and that everything she touched — furniture, clothing, other people — became impure. Twelve years of chronic hemorrhage meant twelve years of ritual uncleanness, social isolation from communal worship, inability to touch others without making them impure, and exclusion from the synagogue. She was legally untouchable. Approaching a crowd and touching a teacher's clothing was a serious violation of ritual law and a significant social risk.
26: Medical treatment in the first century combined Hippocratic practice (diet, exercise, bloodletting, herbal remedies) with folk medicine and temple healing. Jewish women suffering from hemorrhage would have had access to physicians whose treatments for such conditions included astringent herbs, cauterization, and various other remedies — none of which were effective for chronic internal bleeding. The observation that she spent all she had and grew worse is not a polemic against medicine but a narrative establishing that human remedy was exhausted before she came to Jesus.
27: She "heard about Jesus" — the stories of his healing activity (1:28, 1:45, 3:8) had circulated widely enough to reach someone in her social situation, probably transmitted by others who had been healed or had witnessed healings. Her approach from behind (opisthen, in the crowd) is strategic: touching Jesus from the front would require him to look at her, recognize her condition, and be seen to permit or deny contact. Approaching from behind in a crowd allows her to touch and potentially withdraw undetected — the act of someone who knows she is not supposed to be there at all.
28: "If I just touch his clothes, I will be healed" — her reasoning reflects a widely attested ancient belief in the therapeutic power associated with holy people, transmitted through physical contact with garments, relics, or shadows. The Egyptian magician Papyri record analogous formulas. Jewish tradition associated healing power with the fringe (tzitzit) of a garment (Mal 4:2 LXX: "healing in his wings/fringes"). Her reasoning is neither superstitious nor mechanical — she is attributing a specific power to a specific person and seeking contact with it.
29: The healing is immediate and proprioceptive — she knew in her body that she was freed from her suffering. The word for "freed" (iaomai) is the standard Greek word for medical healing; the word for "suffering" (mastix, literally "scourge" or "whip") was used metaphorically for chronic disease. Her physical certainty precedes any word from Jesus. The hemorrhage stops at the moment of contact, before any exchange — establishing that the power transfer happened through the touch, not through a verbal declaration of faith.
30: "Power had gone out from him" (dynamis ex autou exelthein) — dynamis in Greek means both physical power and miraculous power. The phrase implies that healing is not a technique Jesus performs but a power that flows from him, and that even involuntary contact can trigger it. Jesus's question "Who touched my clothes?" is not ignorance but an invitation to disclosure — as v.32 confirms, he is looking around deliberately. Mark's account preserves this as a spontaneous, real-time event rather than a controlled dispensation of healing.
31: The disciples' response — "the crowd is pressing against you and you ask who touched you?" — reflects genuine puzzlement. In a dense crowd where physical contact is constant, distinguishing one purposeful touch from the general pressure seems impossible. The disciples' practical objection underlines the specificity of what Jesus perceived: not the general contact of a crowd but a particular contact made with particular intent. The distinction between accidental contact and faith-directed touch is apparently perceptible.
32: Jesus "kept looking around to see who had done it" — the imperfect tense suggests sustained, deliberate looking. The woman knew what had happened and knew that Jesus knew something had happened. The subsequent confrontation is not Jesus hunting for a wrongdoer but creating the opportunity for public testimony. The woman's healing was real but secret; Jesus draws her into public acknowledgment, both for her own sake and as a witness to the crowd, including Jairus, who is watching this delay while his daughter dies.
33: The woman comes "knowing what had happened to her" — the Greek participle (eidyia) is emphatic. She is not uncertain or confused; she knows she is healed. She also knows she has violated ritual law by touching an unclean person to an apparently holy man in a public setting. She falls before him "trembling with fear" (phoboumenē kai tremousa) and tells him "the whole truth" (pasan tēn alētheian). The confession requires her to name her condition publicly, before the crowd, including Jairus and his attendants — an act of enormous social and emotional courage.
34: "Daughter" (thygatēr) — not a diminutive but the same form of address used between adults. Jesus addresses a ritually impure, socially excluded woman as daughter, publicly, before a crowd that includes a synagogue ruler. The word carries the weight of belonging: she is not cast out for her violation of ritual purity but welcomed into the family of those who belong to Jesus. "Your faith has healed you" (hē pistis sou sesōken se) uses the perfect tense of sōzō — a state of being saved or healed that is now established. "Go in peace" (hypage eis eirēnēn) echoes the Hebrew priestly blessing formula shalom, a state of wholeness and restored standing.
35: The messengers from Jairus's house arrive while Jesus is still speaking — the delay caused by the woman's healing has cost Jairus his daughter. "Your daughter is dead; why bother the teacher any more?" reflects the practical calculus of grief: death is final, and continuing to impose on a healer for a corpse is pointless. The word "teacher" (didaskalos) is the form of address used for Jesus by outsiders throughout Mark — respectful but not recognizing his full authority. The messengers are not hostile; they are simply reporting what they believe to be true.
36: "Don't be afraid; just believe" — the command echoes 4:40 ("Why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith?") addressed to the disciples in the storm. Jairus is in a position structurally identical to the disciples on the boat: facing a situation that appears beyond remediation, asked to maintain trust in Jesus when circumstances argue against it. The command is not addressed to the messengers (who were not asking for anything) but to Jairus, the only person whose response still matters for what is about to happen.
37: Peter, James, and John constitute the inner circle of the Twelve who witness the uniquely significant events: the Transfiguration (9:2), Gethsemane (14:33), and here, the raising of Jairus's daughter. This selective circle of witnesses follows the pattern of restricted access to divine presence in the OT — not everyone could enter the Holy Place, and not everyone could witness what Moses saw on the mountain. The exclusion of others also prevents crowd interference with a scene requiring quiet and focused faith.
38: Professional mourning was a standard social institution in the ancient Near East and first-century Jewish culture. The Mishnah (m. Ketubbot 4:4) specifies that even the poorest man in Israel was required to hire at least two flute players and one wailing woman for his wife's funeral; wealthier families employed many more. These professionals arrived quickly and began their work before burial. The "commotion, wailing, and crying" Jesus finds at Jairus's house is therefore not merely spontaneous grief but the organized public expression of a death announcement — the mourning infrastructure already deployed.
39: "The child is not dead but asleep" — the statement would have seemed either deceptional or deluded to professional mourners who had just seen the child's body. Whether Jesus means that death itself is, from his perspective, a temporary state (as 1 Cor 15:51 implies), or that the child was in a coma misdiagnosed as death, the result is the same: he treats the situation as reversible. The mourners' laughter (v.40) is the appropriate response of professionals to someone who appears to misunderstand their trade. Jesus then expels them — what is about to happen does not require an audience of unbelievers.
40: The expulsion of the mourners is an act of deliberate social authority in a culture where death rituals were communal and not easily interrupted. Jesus takes charge of the scene: the mourners are put outside (ekbalōn — the same verb used for exorcism), and only the parents and the three disciples are brought in. The restriction creates the intimate setting for the miracle that follows, paralleling Elijah's similar withdrawal in 1 Kgs 17:19-20 (and Elisha's in 2 Kgs 4:33) before raising a child from the dead. Mark's Jewish audience would have caught the prophetic pattern immediately.
41: "Talitha koum" is Aramaic, preserved here in transliteration — one of the few places in the Gospels where the actual Aramaic words of Jesus are retained. Papias (apud Eusebius HE 3.39) records that Mark wrote down Peter's teaching accurately, preserving what Peter said rather than arranging it as a connected discourse. The retention of Aramaic phrases in Mark (also ephphatha in 7:34, abba in 14:36) is consistent with eyewitness memory: Peter remembered and Mark preserved the actual syllables spoken. For a Roman Gentile audience, the Aramaic words required explanation (which Mark provides), but their preservation signals the proximity of the account to its source.
42: The girl was twelve years old — the same number as the hemorrhaging woman's twelve years of illness. The structural parallelism is deliberate: both are daughters (Jairus calls his daughter "my little daughter"; Jesus addresses the hemorrhaging woman as "daughter"), both involve the boundary between life and death (the woman on the edge of social death; the girl on the edge of biological death), and both are restored by Jesus in sequence. The twelve-year span mirrors the girl's entire lifetime: she has been a living person exactly as long as the woman has been in chronic suffering.
43: The strict orders not to let anyone know about this (the Messianic Secret in its sharpest form — resurrection cannot be kept secret for long, but Jesus suppresses it as long as possible) are paired with the practical instruction to give her food. The feeding instruction confirms that the child is biologically alive and functional, not a ghost or vision — and also that she is hungry, because the normal processes of embodied life have resumed. The combination of secrecy command and domestic practicality is characteristically Markan: the miraculous is anchored immediately in the ordinary.
